nuiUbpsAAAAJ.keywords: Networking, Computer Networks, Systems, Mobile Computing, Device-free Passive WiFi Localization, Traffic Shaping, Congestion Control, Packet Scheduling, UAV/Drone Visual Sensing, LEO Satellite Networks
nuiUbpsAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Ahmed Saeed is an **Assistant Professor** in the College of Computing at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group in *networking and systems* (see his homepage: https://www.cc.gatech.edu/~amsmti3). His work sits at the intersection of **computer networks**, **systems**, and *mobile computing*, with a sustained emphasis on <u>device-free passive localization</u>, <u>traffic shaping and congestion control</u>, and <u>scalable network/system mechanisms for datacenters and wide-area networks</u>. Saeed's research combines measurement-driven systems-building, algorithmic design, and formal/automated reasoning to produce practical, deployable techniques for wireless environments, datacenter stacks, and next-generation networking platforms.

---

## Research Areas
Saeed's body of work spans several tightly related areas in networking and systems. In wireless sensing and localization he has pioneered work on **device-free passive (DfP) localization** and motion detection—most notably developing large-scale systems that exploit commodity WLAN infrastructure (e.g., "Nuzzer", "RASID", and "Ichnaea") to localize or detect people who do not carry devices. In networked systems and datacenter networking he has investigated scalable end-host mechanisms for traffic shaping and packet scheduling (e.g., "Carousel", the time-indexed traffic-shaping patents, and "Eiffel"), and explored the interaction between congestion control, overload control, and core scheduling for µs-scale RPCs and microservice fabrics (e.g., "Overload Control for µs-scale RPCs with Breakwater", "Annulus", "Protego", and recent work on joint core scheduling and overload control). He also contributes to formal and automated methods for networking—both tooling and verification—through work such as "Toward formally verifying congestion control behavior" and NSF-style collaborative projects on behavioral analysis of network control protocols. Complementary research threads include mobile visual sensing and UAV-enabled testbeds ("Argus", "Up and away", target coverage papers), spectrum/white-space awareness and geo-database studies, and recent investigations into the performance and modeling of Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks.

---

## Notable Works
- "Nuzzer: A large-scale device-free passive localization system for wireless environments" (2012) — introduced a practical, WLAN-based approach to localize and track entities without attached devices, demonstrating how commodity WiFi infrastructure can be repurposed for large-scale *device-free passive (DfP) localization* (450 citations).
- "RASID: A robust WLAN device-free passive motion detection system" (2012) — developed robust algorithms and system techniques for motion detection using existing wireless deployments, advancing the reliability and applicability of DfP systems in realistic environments (296 citations).
- "Carousel: Scalable traffic shaping at end hosts" (2017) — proposed end-host, scalable traffic-shaping designs that enable practical pacing and rate-limiting for both datacenter and WAN traffic, influencing subsequent work on time-indexed shaping structures and scalable sender-side mechanisms (180 citations).

---

## Academic Background
Ahmed Saeed is an Assistant Professor at Georgia Tech's College of Computing, where he directs a research group working on networking and systems. His publication record (total citations ≈ 1,922) reflects long-term collaborations across wireless sensing, datacenter networking, congestion/overload control, and formal analysis of network algorithms. He has been PI/co-PI on multi-institutional research projects (including NSF-style "Collaborative Research: CNS Core" efforts) and holds multiple systems and method disclosures related to scalable traffic shaping. Prior to his faculty appointment he conducted extensive research in mobile and wireless networking and systems (details and CV available on his homepage). Saeed maintains active collaborations with academic and industry partners on problems ranging from UAV-enabled sensing and TV white-space awareness to LEO-satellite network characterization and high-performance end-host network stacks.
